content.problemStatement:
You are a traveler and you are at a junction of 222 paths - one leads to eternal happiness, the other to certain doom.

There are a total of N+MN + MN+M people, NNN of which always tell the truth, while the remaining MMM may tell the truth or lie. All of them are aware of which path leads to where.

You don't know who is a truth teller and who is not. You will ask some randomly chosen XXX people the following question :


Which among the 222 paths leads to eternal happiness?
Is it possible for you to be sure which paths leads to eternal happiness?

If it is possible, find the minimal XXX where you can be sure of the path regardless of which XXX people you end up asking. Otherwise, print −1-1−1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM - the number of truth tellers, and the number of people who may or may not tell the truth.


Output Format
For each test case, output the following :



−1-1−1 if there is no way to figure out which path is the path to happiness.
Otherwise, print an integer X (1≤X≤N+M)X \ (1 \le X \le N + M)X (1≤X≤N+M), where XXX is the minimum integer such that you can be sure of the path after asking any XXX people.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111 : It can be proven that asking 222 people is not sufficient as one of them could be the liar and give a different answer than the truth teller, and you can't know who is lying and who is not.
With all 333 responses, it is possible to figure out the correct path. For example, suppose the responses were Path 111, Path 111 and Path 222. Then you know that the correct path is Path 111.
Test case 222 : It can be proven to be impossible to figure out the correct path.
